utterances: Joining us today from our New York Bureau are E.R.

Shipp, professor of journalism at Hofstra University; and Michael Meyers, executive director of the New York Civil Rights Coalition; plus Glenn Loury, professor of economics at Brown University is at member station WRNI in Providence, Rhode Island.
Welcome, and let's pick up on something that we started yesterday on the roundtable, we just didn't have enough time, this is Iran.
Yesterday, the nation of Iran proudly announced that they'd found a way to produce nuclear fuel on an industrial scale.
This comes just after reports that the U.S.
is considering military action against the nation.
Now, the National Security Council announced that the U.S.
would work with the U.N.
to deal with the significant threat posed by the regime's efforts to acquire nuclear weapons.
That's a quote.
Glenn, let me start with you.
This is, depending on whether you are the president of Iran or not, good news.
And, what does it mean that the U.S.
has ramped up talk of military action just at the time that Iran is publicly crowing about this new technology?
Well, it's a very serious threat there.
And of course, we need to be thinking about how to respond to it.
I'm not surprised that there might be planning of this kind of contingency going on, but all reports are that Iran a long way, years away, from actually having the weapon.
And I think the evidence supports the view that the administration will mainly be trying to take a diplomatic tack here.
Military force is an option of last resort, I'm sure.
E.R., this is a technology that Iran says it's only going to use to produce energy.
Are we supposed to believe that?
Professor E.R.
SHIPP (Journalism Professor, Hofstra University): Well, I'm not an expert on Iran or its intents, but it makes a good point.

As a member, in fairly good standing in the international community, it says it has a right to produce nuclear power, and it says it's also adhering to various treaty provisions, which restrict what it can do, which makes Iran obligated to permit inspectors to come in and examine what's going on.
Part of this seems to me to be a big dance routine going on now.
The U.S.
has to sound tough because some of the senior advisors have said that it hasn't sounded tough enough in dealing with Iran, and Iran's president needs to look equally as tough in the world stage.
Michael, at this point, what is the wisest course for the U.S.?
But even private schools, and religious schools, ought to spell out their standards so people can have some reliable basis from which to make decisions about admission.
It's difficult to know.
It's a very underreported phenomenon, as you and your listeners know.
Most people never go to the authorities after they've been sexually assaulted.

The best guess that we have is that between 20 and 25 percent of women experience some sort of sexual assault or attempted assault by the age of 18, and that about five to 15 percent of men had similar experiences, you know, before the age or by the age of 18.

So certainly it is - appears to be a large phenomenon, something that is experienced by a number of people, the vast majority of whom are not going to go on to engage in inappropriate or illegal sexual behaviors.

But that is still a - that's a big problem, especially if, as you said earlier, you're not talking about - you know, many people have the experience of, say, you know, maybe they're working at a fast food restaurant and, you know, there are, you know, they are cashiers, say, at the fast-food restaurant in the neighborhood, their after-school job, and there's some, you know, a jerk who likes to come in and say, you know, inappropriate things to a person.
If you're taking that out of the mix, it's still a significant problem.
It is still a significant problem, and certainly more people probably experience the kind of harassment that you are mentioning.

And that I think, you know, we might characterize, you know, sexually explicit e-mails that are unwanted that are sent to teenagers or minors, those might perhaps more fall under the context of harassment more than abuse.
Do you know - do we know to whom this occurs most often, you know, age, gender, other circumstances?
We know that girls are far more likely to be abused, sexually abused, than boys, although boys still certainly are sexually abused and the effects on both genders seems to be similar.
Younger children, children who are just entering school age - five-year-olds, six-year-olds, seven-year-olds - are at increased risk.
They're coming out of their homes.
They're away from caregivers who've bonded with them, sometimes for the first time.
And those children present, you know, very easy targets for people who are sexually interested in children.
You know, certainly there are quite a few people who are interested in physically mature teenagers.
We know, for example, that a substantial portion of adult men will show some sexual interest in teenagers.

Most of these men never act on those impulses or those interests, and then the question becomes, well, why would someone - you know, for example, why would a 50-year-old career politician, or anyone else for that matter, send sexually explicit or suggestive notes to teenage boys or to other teenagers?
And there's a number of potential drivers for this behavior, and when I talk about drivers, I'm talking about, you know, things that appear to drive or cause inappropriate sexual behavior.
And those can include things like cognitive distortions: not believing that it's a bad thing to send these kind of messages to teenagers.
And we've got groups like NAMBLA that advocate sexual relationships between adults and minors, and the members of those organizations have cognitive distortions around having sex with children.
They ignore the danger there.
Another driver would be narcissism, you know, basically caring more about your own wants and needs than the wants and needs of others, or a callous indifference to others.

And speaking of Mr. Foley's situation, which of course we don't know what happened, we don't know what ultimately is going to be the case, but, you know, I would say in general politics is probably a profession that selects for narcissism, much like we might say professional football is a profession that selects for a level of aggressiveness that's higher than we see in the average population.

Other drivers of sexual behavior that we know are linked include a history of child maltreatment, and that includes child sexual assault, child physical assault, child neglect; any kind of that type of negative experience as a child have been linked to a host of poor outcomes.
Some children do fine.
But if you are sexually or physically assaulted as a child, you're at increased risk for just about every bad thing.
And I think the comments that you've played by Dr. Freed(ph) spoke to that.

Your at increased risk for suicide and substance abuse and depression and PTSD and doing poorly in school, doing poorly with your job, divorce, STDs, a whole host of things, including you're at increased risk for criminal and sexually criminal behavior.
But the vast majority of sexual assault victims and physical assault victims never go on to become sex offenders.
So there's not - you know, abuse does not lead to offending.
Is that a misconception you want to clear up?
I mean do you have a sense that most - that there's a perception out there that if you are yourself a victim, you are likely to become a perpetrator?
Is that something you feel a need to clarify, that that's not the case?
Yes, and I think there's a good reason for that.
I think there's two reasons for it.
First of all, I think it offers a heuristic for the rest of us to understand how could someone engage in despicable behavior and behavior that the rest of us would never consider doing?
I assume that the vote's being jiggled and chiseled, whether they do it one way or another.
And I mean, come on, we're New York.
We're the Big Apple.
I actually heard something ringing and I thought, oh, that must be somewhere else.
And if you know that someone has a history of sexual abuse, I think it offers you a type of explanation for how that might have happened.
Maybe there's this cycle of abuse where you're a victim and you become a victimizer, and we know that simply is not the case for most offenders.
The second reason I think that that seems to come up a lot is that offenders themselves have learned that saying that they have a history of sexual assault engenders some sympathy for them.

And, you know, one research study that I did of men who were in treatment for sexual abuse - so these were convicted sex offenders - showed that, you know, before treatment started they were asked on a questionnaire had they ever been assaulted, and a large percentage said yes, they had histories of childhood sexual assault.
That question was added to polygraph assessment, and the number of men indicating a history of child sexual assault was cut in about half, and so it went down markedly.
And so I think what was happening was these men, you know, they knew people wanted an explanation for their behavior.
And it's easier to say I was abused as a child than it is to say I'm sexually attracted to children or I'm sexually attracted to teenagers.
I want to see if I can just bring a caller in right here.
And let's go to New York City and Pat(ph).
Pat, what's your question?
Yeah, I was sexually abused as a young teenager and I've never felt the need to go out and shoot up a school because of my misfortune.
I've led a productive adult life as a lawyer and have my own children.
And I just wonder what's the deciding factor there?
And I'll take my answer off the air.
Thank you.
Okay, Pat, thanks for calling.
That's a great question, and it raises two points.
First of all, if abuse led to offending, our offenders would overwhelmingly be female.
And that's not the case.
And so, yeah, so the vast majority of child sexual assault victims never go on to engage in anything like this kind of behavior.
So when we look at drivers of behavior, we distinguish between drivers that occurred long ago, distal drivers, and drivers that are in close temporal proximity to the bad behavior.

So knowing that someone was molested when they were 10 and now they're 50 and engaging in bad behavior, or even 30 and engaging in bad behavior, that might be interesting, but it's not especially helpful.
You know, in terms of why would someone send inappropriate messages that could end their career, you know, there's any number of potential drivers.
You know, again, I was speaking about cognitive distortions and narcissism...
I'm sorry.
Professor, we're going to need to take a short break.
We're talking with Elizabeth Latourneau about the legacy of childhood sexual abuse, how victims cope after it happens.

Let's go to Laura, who is in - is it Moraga, California, Laura?
Yes.
Okay.
Laura, what's your question, please?
I had a question about balancing the severity of the abuse with the secretiveness.
I know in these day and ages there's lots of education as to, you know, be careful about being improperly touched or whatever and tell someone immediately.

In my own case, it took 20 years for me to tell my mother that I was - had been - it was only three incidents, two or three - an uncle of mine while I was getting "free piano lessons," which were not free in the end.
And it took 20 years.
But, you know, the kind of symptoms I had - sleepwalking - at the time that came right after that, nobody really kind of, you know, took notice.
And I think once I was able to tell my mother and she was able to confront this aunt, my healing started.
And, you know, I think for years - and so, you know, I had to completely submerge for 20 years.
And I just - I don't know - and the reason - I can tell you the reason I didn't tell anyone is because this was a beloved aunt, my father's very close sister.
And I knew if I told it would have been - hell would have broken loose probably and would have destroyed this bond in the family.

And so I was protecting, you know, my aunt with this husband who probably abused quite a few children because he was able to give piano lessons in his home and my aunt was out working, and he had complete freedom to do that.
Anyway, my question is about not telling someone immediately and that - the toll of that...
Laura, thank you...
...and chances of recovery.
